Github618
Getting StartedMotionAI Agents

Concepts

Motion PatternsEasing ReferenceSpring Config

Motion

Spring Config

Springs simulate physics. Instead of a fixed duration and easing curve, a spring's timing emerges from stiffness, damping, and mass — making motion feel alive rather than scripted.

Spring vs tween

Use a tween when you need exact timing control (page transitions, synced animations). Use a spring when the motion should feel interactive and physical — especially for anything that responds to user input.

TSXcomponent.tsx
// Tween — you control duration exactly
transition={{ duration: 0.22, ease: EASE_OUT }}

// Spring — duration is emergent from physics; feels alive
transition={{ type: "spring", stiffness: 400, damping: 30 }}

Parameters

stiffnessnumber

How strong the spring pulls toward the target. Higher = snappier. Typical range: 100–800.

dampingnumber

How much the spring resists oscillation. Lower = more bouncy, higher = critically damped. Typical range: 15–60.

massnumber

Inertia of the element. Rarely changed from the default of 1. Increase for a heavier, slower feel.

bounce0–1

Shorthand for combined stiffness + damping. 0 = no overshoot, 1 = max wobble. Use with duration.

durationseconds

Approximate settle time. Only meaningful when using the bounce API — not combined with stiffness/damping.

restDeltanumber

Distance threshold below which the spring is considered settled. Default 0.01.

Bounce shorthand

Framer Motion 11 introduced a simpler API: set bounce (0–1) and duration instead of stiffness and damping. Good for quick prototyping; reach for the explicit params when you need fine control.

TSXcomponent.tsx
// Short-hand bounce API (Framer Motion 11+)
// bounce: 0 → critically damped (no overshoot)
// bounce: 1 → maximum wobble
transition={{ type: "spring", duration: 0.4, bounce: 0.25 }}

Presets

Four named presets cover the majority of use cases in Kinetic UI. Define them as constants and import where needed.

Snappystiffness: 500, damping: 35

Tight, immediate, no overshoot

Buttons, toggles, checkboxes, small interactive elements

Gentlestiffness: 200, damping: 28

Smooth, relaxed, slight overshoot

Layout shifts, list reordering, content reveals

Bouncystiffness: 350, damping: 18

Playful, clear overshoot, wobbles

Badges, notification dots, celebration moments

Stiffstiffness: 700, damping: 50

Near-instant, physically rigid

Drag handles, sliders, things that must feel 1:1 with input

TSXspring-snappy.tsx
// Snappy — tight response for buttons, toggles, and small elements
const SPRING_SNAPPY = { type: "spring", stiffness: 500, damping: 35 } as const;

<motion.div whileTap={{ scale: 0.95 }} transition={SPRING_SNAPPY} />
TSXspring-gentle.tsx
// Gentle — smooth, relaxed for layout shifts and reveals
const SPRING_GENTLE = { type: "spring", stiffness: 200, damping: 28 } as const;

<motion.div
  layout
  transition={SPRING_GENTLE}
/>
TSXspring-bouncy.tsx
// Bouncy — playful overshoot for icons, badges, and delight moments
const SPRING_BOUNCY = { type: "spring", stiffness: 350, damping: 18 } as const;

<motion.div
  animate={{ scale: 1 }}
  initial={{ scale: 0 }}
  transition={SPRING_BOUNCY}
/>

Layout animations

Springs are the only sensible choice for layout animations. Tweens with fixed durations feel mismatched when elements move different distances — springs naturally scale timing to distance.

TSXlist.tsx
// Recommended layout spring — fast enough to feel responsive
//   but smooth enough not to jar
<motion.ul layout>
  {items.map((item) => (
    <motion.li
      key={item.id}
      layout
      transition={{ type: "spring", stiffness: 300, damping: 30 }}
    />
  ))}
</motion.ul>

Rules

1

Never use stiffness/damping and duration/bounce together — they're two separate APIs.

2

Damping below ~15 produces visible oscillation; use it only for deliberate bouncy effects.

3

Don't use springs for opacity — opacity has no physical analogy and the overshoot reads as a flicker.

4

Keep mass at 1 unless you're intentionally simulating something heavy.

5

Springs don't have a fixed end time. If you need to chain animations with exact timing, use a tween.
